401: Philosophy of science thinker who proposed the falsifiability criterion as the hallmark of scientific theories and championed the open society against totalitarianism.
402: Linguist and political critic regarded as the father of generative grammar, which revolutionized the scientific study of language.
403: French philosopher who analyzed the relationships between power, knowledge, and discipline in modern society, influencing sociology, cultural studies, and political theory.
404: French philosopher and founder of deconstruction, a method of critical analysis that challenges fixed meanings in texts and structures.
405: Political philosopher and author of 'A Theory of Justice,' a landmark work of egalitarian liberalism built around the concept of justice as fairness.
406: Austrian economist and philosopher, Nobel laureate, and leading intellectual reference of 20th-century liberalism and free-market thought.
407: British economist and father of modern macroeconomics, whose theories on government intervention and aggregate demand shaped 20th-century economic policy.
408: Nobel Prize-winning economist, leader of the Chicago School, and foremost advocate of monetarism and free-market capitalism.
409: Anglo-American poet and author of 'The Waste Land,' a defining work of literary modernism; awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1948.
410: Irish novelist and author of 'Ulysses' and 'Finnegans Wake,' landmark works of stream-of-consciousness modernist fiction.
411: Irish poet and Nobel laureate in Literature (1923), central figure of the Irish Literary Revival and one of the greatest English-language poets of the 20th century.
412: Irish playwright and author of 'Waiting for Godot,' a cornerstone of the Theatre of the Absurd; awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1969.
413: Philologist and author of 'The Lord of the Rings' and 'The Hobbit,' foundational works of modern fantasy literature.
414: British writer and Christian apologist, author of 'The Chronicles of Narnia' and influential works of theological fiction and non-fiction.
415: British novelist and author of 'Brave New World,' a classic 20th-century dystopia depicting a technologically controlled future society.
416: Father of modern science fiction, author of 'The Time Machine' and 'The War of the Worlds,' whose visionary stories shaped the genre.
417: Queen of crime fiction and creator of detectives Hercule Poirot and Miss Marple, the best-selling mystery novelist of all time.
418: Creator of Sherlock Holmes, the most famous detective in literary history, and one of the most widely read British authors.
419: British author of beloved children's classics including 'Matilda' and 'Charlie and the Chocolate Factory,' known for his dark humor and imaginative storytelling.
420: Author of the 'Harry Potter' series, one of the best-selling and most culturally influential works of fiction in contemporary history.
421: Contemporary master of horror fiction and author of 'It,' 'The Shining,' and 'Carrie,' one of the most prolific and popular novelists of his era.
422: African-American Nobel laureate in Literature (1993) and author of 'Beloved,' whose novels explore the legacy of slavery and the Black experience in America.
423: Author of 'To Kill a Mockingbird,' a Pulitzer Prize-winning classic that confronts racial injustice in the American South.
424: Author of 'The Catcher in the Rye,' an iconic coming-of-age novel that became a defining text of American adolescent experience.
425: Central figure of the Beat Generation and author of 'On the Road,' a seminal novel of countercultural restlessness and spontaneous prose.
426: American poet and novelist, author of 'The Bell Jar,' known for her confessional verse and her unflinching depiction of mental illness.
427: Argentine novelist of the Latin American Boom and author of 'Hopscotch,' an experimental work that can be read in multiple orders.
428: Peruvian novelist of the Latin American Boom and Nobel laureate in Literature (2010), known for works exploring politics, power, and identity.
429: Chilean author of 'The House of the Spirits' and numerous international bestsellers blending magical realism with political history.
430: Mexican poet and essayist, Nobel laureate in Literature (1990), whose work meditates on Mexican identity, modernity, and love.
431: Spanish poet of the Generation of '98 and author of 'Fields of Castile,' celebrated for his lyrical evocations of the Castilian landscape.
432: Spanish poet and Nobel laureate in Literature (1956), author of 'Platero and I,' a lyrical prose poem considered a masterpiece of Spanish literature.
433: Soviet dissident writer, Nobel laureate in Literature, and author of 'The Gulag Archipelago,' a monumental account of the Soviet forced-labor camp system.
434: Russian poet and novelist, author of 'Doctor Zhivago,' who was forced under Soviet pressure to decline the Nobel Prize in Literature awarded to him in 1958.
435: Russian-American novelist and author of 'Lolita' and 'Pale Fire,' celebrated for his intricate prose style and literary playfulness.
436: Portuguese novelist, Nobel laureate in Literature (1998), and author of 'Blindness,' a parable about societal collapse told with minimal punctuation.
437: Portuguese poet known for writing under multiple literary personas called heteronyms, including Álvaro de Campos and Ricardo Reis, a cornerstone of literary modernism.
438: Italian novelist and author of 'Invisible Cities' and the 'Our Ancestors' trilogy, celebrated for his playful, philosophical, and fantastical fiction.
439: Italian semiotician and novelist, author of 'The Name of the Rose,' a medieval detective story and bestselling work of postmodern fiction.
440: Auschwitz survivor and writer, author of 'If This Is a Man,' a sober and lucid testimony to the experience of the Nazi concentration camps.
441: German-Swiss novelist and Nobel laureate (1946), author of 'Siddhartha' and 'Steppenwolf,' whose works blend Eastern spirituality with European psychology.
442: German novelist and Nobel laureate (1929), author of 'The Magic Mountain' and 'Buddenbrooks,' towering works of European literary modernism.
443: German playwright and creator of epic theatre, author of 'Mother Courage and Her Children,' whose Brechtian techniques aimed to provoke critical thought in audiences.
444: Austrian writer and author of 'The World of Yesterday' and numerous acclaimed biographies, one of the most widely read European authors of the early 20th century.
445: Austrian-Bohemian poet and author of the 'Duino Elegies,' considered one of the greatest lyric poets in the German language.
446: French painter, leader of Fauvism, and one of the great colorists of the 20th century, celebrated for his bold use of pure color.
447: Russian painter and theorist, pioneer of abstract art and one of the founders of non-representational painting in the early 20th century.
448: French artist, father of conceptual art and the readymade, whose provocative works permanently altered the definition of art.
449: Belgian Surrealist painter and author of 'The Treachery of Images,' famous for thought-provoking juxtapositions that challenge perception and meaning.
450: Mexican muralist and central figure of the muralist movement, known for his large-scale public frescoes and his marriage to Frida Kahlo.
451: American artist and leading figure of Pop Art, celebrated for his silkscreen prints of consumer goods and celebrities such as Campbell's Soup Cans and Marilyn Monroe.
452: American painter and key figure of Abstract Expressionism, famous for his drip painting technique that placed him at the forefront of the New York avant-garde.
453: New York neo-expressionist painter who emerged from the graffiti scene and became one of the most celebrated artists of the 1980s.
454: Contemporary Japanese artist celebrated worldwide for her immersive polka-dot installations and pumpkin sculptures, a leading figure of avant-garde art.
455: Anonymous British street artist and global symbol of politically engaged urban art, whose stenciled works appear on walls and public spaces worldwide.
456: Russian composer and pianist, one of the last great Romantics, celebrated for his sweeping piano concertos and symphonies.
457: Soviet composer of fifteen symphonies, whose music navigated the constraints and terrors of the Stalinist era with coded emotional intensity.
458: Austrian composer and conductor, creator of monumental post-Romantic symphonies that pushed orchestral music to new expressive extremes.
459: American composer and conductor, author of 'West Side Story,' who bridged classical and popular music and became one of the most celebrated musicians of his era.
460: Trumpeter and singer widely regarded as the foundational figure of jazz, whose virtuosity and charisma shaped the music of the 20th century.
461: Trumpeter and composer who pioneered multiple jazz styles including bebop, cool jazz, and fusion, making him one of the most influential musicians in history.
462: Jazz saxophonist and central figure of modal jazz and free jazz, whose recordings such as 'A Love Supreme' are considered milestones of the genre.
463: Known as the 'First Lady of Song,' she is considered the greatest jazz vocalist of the 20th century, renowned for her pure tone and impeccable phrasing.
464: Jazz singer with an unmistakable voice, celebrated for emotional depth and for recording 'Strange Fruit,' an anthem against racial violence.
465: Singer and actor known as 'Ol' Blue Eyes,' an icon of American popular music and the quintessential interpreter of the Great American Songbook.
466: 'The King of Rock and Roll,' one of the most influential musicians of the 20th century who helped bring rock and roll to a global audience.
467: American singer-songwriter and Nobel laureate in Literature (2016), whose folk and rock songs became anthems of social change and poetic expression.
468: Jamaican singer and global ambassador of reggae music and Rastafarianism, whose songs of peace, love, and resistance transcend generations.
469: 'The King of Pop,' one of the best-selling and most influential musical artists in history, known for his electrifying performances and music videos.
470: Multi-instrumentalist singer who innovated across funk, pop, and rock, widely regarded as one of the most gifted and prolific musicians of his generation.
471: Chameleon-like British rock icon, pioneer of glam rock, and one of the most influential artists in pop music history, known for reinventing himself across decades.
472: 'The Queen of Pop,' a defining figure of popular culture since the 1980s who continuously reinvented her musical and visual identity.
473: Revolutionary guitarist considered the greatest in rock history, who transformed the electric guitar into an expressive instrument of extraordinary range and power.
474: Leader and co-founder of Nirvana and the defining voice of the grunge movement that dominated rock music in the early 1990s.
475: 'The Queen of Soul,' one of the most powerful and influential voices in 20th-century music, celebrated for her gospel-rooted performances and civil rights activism.
476: Greek soprano widely regarded as one of the most influential opera singers of the 20th century, renowned for her dramatic intensity and vocal mastery.
477: Italian tenor, one of the most celebrated and beloved opera singers of the 20th century, known for his powerful voice and popular appeal.
478: Argentine bandoneon player and composer, creator of 'nuevo tango,' who transformed traditional tango into an internationally recognized art form.
479: British director and 'Master of Suspense,' creator of 'Psycho' and 'Vertigo,' whose innovative techniques profoundly influenced the art of cinema.
480: American director and actor, creator of 'Citizen Kane,' widely considered one of the greatest films ever made and a landmark of cinematic technique.
481: Japanese director and author of 'Seven Samurai' and 'Rashomon,' one of the most influential filmmakers in the history of cinema.
482: Italian director and author of 'La Dolce Vita' and '8½,' a master of cinematic imagination whose work defined European art cinema.
483: Swedish director and leading figure of auteur cinema, known for existentially and spiritually probing works such as 'The Seventh Seal.'
484: Spanish director and pioneer of Surrealist cinema, author of 'Un Chien Andalou,' whose provocative films challenged bourgeois and religious conventions.
485: Spanish filmmaker from La Mancha and central figure of contemporary Spanish cinema, known for his vivid, emotionally charged dramas.
486: American director of 'Taxi Driver' and 'Goodfellas,' a master of visceral storytelling and one of Hollywood's most acclaimed filmmakers.
487: American director of 'E.T.,' 'Jurassic Park,' and 'Schindler's List,' one of the most commercially successful and celebrated filmmakers in cinema history.
488: American director of 'Pulp Fiction' and 'Kill Bill,' known for his non-linear narratives, sharp dialogue, and eclectic pop-culture references.
489: British director of 'Memento,' the Dark Knight trilogy, and 'Inception,' renowned for complex narratives that blur the line between reality and illusion.
490: Actress and cultural icon of classic Hollywood, whose image became one of the most recognizable symbols of 20th-century popular culture.
491: British actress and star of 'Breakfast at Tiffany's,' celebrated for her elegance on screen and her humanitarian work as a UNICEF Goodwill Ambassador.
492: Actor and martial artist who popularized martial arts cinema in the West, becoming a global icon of physical mastery and philosophical discipline.
493: Brazilian footballer, three-time World Cup champion, widely considered one of the greatest players in the history of the sport.
494: Argentine footballer, 1986 World Cup champion and one of the most legendary figures in football history, celebrated for both brilliance and controversy.
495: Argentine footballer, eight-time Ballon d'Or winner and 2022 World Cup champion, widely regarded as the greatest player of his generation.
496: Portuguese footballer, five-time Ballon d'Or winner and all-time leading scorer for national teams, one of the greatest footballers of his era.
497: Dutch footballer and manager, father of 'Total Football' and architect of the modern style of play at FC Barcelona.
498: Basketball player, six-time NBA champion, widely considered the greatest basketball player of all time and a defining global sports icon.
499: American boxer and three-time heavyweight world champion, celebrated as much for his political activism and refusal of military service as for his brilliance in the ring.
500: Jamaican sprinter, world record holder in the 100 m and 200 m, and eight-time Olympic gold medalist, the fastest man in recorded history.
